title: Nettle compress for mild eczema
subtitle: An astringent, anti-inflammatory compress for weep-free eczema patches
excerpt: A cooled nettle leaf infusion applied as a compress to dry, itchy, non-weeping eczema patches. Nettle's astringent and anti-inflammatory action on the skin surface has a long traditional history for surface skin conditions. Supportive measure only, not a treatment for eczema.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve, A Modern Herbal (1931), nettle entry, astringent and anti-inflammatory topical properties, traditional use for skin conditions. Traditional European domestic use of nettle for skin inflammation, documented in household herbals.

content.text: A cooled nettle leaf infusion soaked into a clean cloth and applied as a compress to dry, itchy, non-weeping eczema patches. Nettle is

and calms surface swelling. The dried leaf infusion contains silica, flavonoids, and other plant parts that ease redness and quiet the itch on dry eczema patches. Traditional use is for dry, non-weeping patches only. Weeping or infected eczema needs medical care. The compress is simple, cheap, and worth trying during a mild flare before reaching for a steroid cream.

attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a medical professional for ongoing or serious symptoms.

This is a supportive preparation for mild, dry, non-weeping eczema patches only. Do not apply to broken, cracked, weeping, or infected skin.

Patch test first: apply a small amount of the cooled infusion to the inner forearm and wait a day before using on an eczema area. A small number of people with sensitive skin react to nettle preparations.

Nettle compress is a supportive addition, not a replacement for prescribed treatment. If your eczema needs steroid cream or another prescription, keep using that treatment and talk to your doctor before adding herbal preparations.

See a medical professional for eczema that is weeping, crusting, very painful, infected, worsening, or spreading. Same goes for any eczema in a child under one year.
attrs.title: Important safety notes

items.name: Dried nettle leaf
items.prepNote: about 1 heaped teaspoon of dried leaf

items.name: Water
items.prepNote: just boiled, then cooled completely before applying to the skin

content.text: Place {{dried-nettle-leaf}} of dried nettle leaf in the teapot. Pour {{water}} of just-boiled water over and lid immediately. Steep for 10 minutes. Strain through a sieve into a clean bowl. Allow to cool completely to room temperature. Soak a piece of clean muslin or cotton in the cool infusion, wring out the excess, and apply gently to the eczema patch. Hold in place for five to ten minutes, re-soaking the cloth as it warms. Do not rub, dab and press only.

Apply the compress once or twice daily during a mild eczema flare. If the skin is responding well after three to five days, continue as needed. If there is no improvement after one week, or if the eczema is worsening, see your GP.
When NOT to use this remedy

Do not apply to weeping, cracked, or infected skin. Always patch test before use on inflamed skin. See your doctor for moderate-to-severe eczema, infected eczema, or eczema in children under one year. This is a supportive measure, continue any prescribed treatment.
